evaluation.manager_summary: This call captured basic intake information (insurance, substance type, treatment history) but contained critical compliance violations including explicit treatment outcome promises and success guarantees. The agent missed opportunities for empathy in the opening, did not screen for urgency or severity indicators, and provided vague next steps without specific timing or ownership. Immediate coaching needed on compliance language and intake fundamentals.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: The agent provided minimal acknowledgment of the caller's distress. The response 'I understand. How long has this been going on?' at 18s was perfunctory and immediately transitioned to data collection without acknowledging the difficulty of the situation or the caller's stated worry.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Slow down in the first 60 seconds. Acknowledge the courage it takes to call and validate the caller's concern before moving to questions.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: The agent collected carrier name, member ID, and group number correctly. However, verification was framed as a simple 'we'll verify and let you know' without hedging coverage statements or explaining the verification step as part of the process toward help.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Frame verification as 'our benefits team will confirm what's covered' to avoid creating expectation before verification is complete.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: The agent captured substance type (cocaine), duration (two years), and frequency (daily), plus prior treatment history. However, critical severity indicators were missing: no questions about last use, withdrawal symptoms, or co-occurring mental health concerns.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Add questions about last use, withdrawal symptoms, and any mental health concerns to better understand clinical urgency and treatment fit.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: The agent did not actively probe for urgency signals despite daily use being disclosed. No questions about withdrawal, safety, living situation, or suicidal ideation. The caller expressed readiness, but urgency was not assessed or triaged appropriately.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Ask about withdrawal symptoms, current living situation safety, and any immediate concerns to properly triage urgency level.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Caller is the patient themselves, confirmed by first-person disclosure of substance use.

The caller raised a cost concern ('A little. I don't know what insurance covers'). The agent acknowledged it briefly and offered reassurance ('Don't worry, we'll get you taken care of'), but did not provide concrete alternatives or explain the benefits verification process clearly.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: When cost concerns arise, explain the benefits verification process and mention alternatives like payment plans or financial assistance if coverage is insufficient.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: The next step (admissions team callback) was stated, but timing was vague ('probably in the next day or so'), and when the caller pressed for specificity, the agent deflected ('I'll put you on the list'). No specific timing or ownership was clarified.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Provide a specific timeframe (e.g., 'within 24 hours') and confirm who will call and what the caller should expect in that conversation.

dimension_scores.ai_rationale: Multiple severe compliance violations: At 28s, the agent made an explicit outcome promise ('our 30-day program gets people better. You'll come out of here a completely different person'), and at 74s repeated it ('this time will be different, I promise. Our program has an excellent success rate'). These are direct treatment success guarantees that constitute unauthorized practice claims.
dimension_scores.coaching_note: Never promise treatment outcomes. Replace with process-focused language: 'Our clinical team will design a plan that fits your needs' and defer all outcome questions to licensed staff.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I'm calling because I've been struggling with cocaine use and my family is really worried about me. I'm worried about me too.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I understand. How long has this been going on?

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Okay. We have a really great program here and I can tell you — our 30-day program gets people better. You'll come out of here a completely different person.

evidence_anchors.speaker: agent
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Absolutely. Do you have insurance?

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Yes, CoreFirst Insurance.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Member ID BP61519698, group GRP-932514.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I've been struggling with cocaine use and my family is really worried about me.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: About two years. It's daily now.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I did a 30-day residential about three years ago.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: About two years. It's daily now.

evidence_anchors.speaker: caller
evidence_anchors.text_snippet: I hope so. I'm ready to commit.

evidence_anchors.text_snippet: Someone from our admissions team will call you. Probably in the next day or so.
